briefing.opening: Good morning. We will now begin the climate briefing for March 17, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Alarms Sounding as Climate Crisis Engulfs the World
sections.content: Today's reports clearly indicate a global escalation in the severity of climate change. An unprecedented record-breaking heatwave that struck the Western U.S. and California is causing difficulties in maintaining the freshness of agricultural products [5] and is expected to incur billions of dollars in costs for Minnesota [43], leading to significant economic repercussions. In ecosystems, erosion in the Sundarbans [47], the endangered status of a flower [44], and potent gas emissions from pig farms contributing to climate change [60] have been observed. Warnings have also emerged that climate change could lead to widespread inactivity and fatalities [71, 77]. Experts caution that climate change poses a greater risk to the Arctic than military threats [75], and it has even been referred to as the latest weapon of war [61]. Amidst this gravity, policy and economic challenges are also piling up, with Louisiana lawmakers restricting climate change mitigation bills [51] and New Jersey debating whether to impose climate change costs on major oil companies [59].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Efforts Towards a Carbon-Neutral Society: Domestic and International Policy Progress
sections.content: The transition to carbon neutrality in response to the climate crisis is materializing through various policy efforts both domestically and internationally. The Korean National Assembly is strengthening carbon neutrality legislation to accelerate the shift to ESG management [11]. The Daegu Metropolitan Office of Education is leading carbon-neutral practice-focused environmental education during an era of high oil prices and energy crisis, achieving participation from 32,000 individuals [12, 18, 19]. At the local government level, the eco-friendly transformation of industrial complexes is gaining momentum, exemplified by the launch of Yangsan City's Carbon Neutrality Action Group [8] and the promotion of a 130.2 billion KRW carbon-neutral model project in Gumi National Industrial Complex [9, 13, 26]. Furthermore, Siheung City and Gyeonggi Province are holding citizen-participatory climate policy town hall meetings [10, 27], and Changwon City officials are promoting efforts to normalize carbon neutrality in daily life, such as reducing paper use, using tumblers, and practicing vegetarianism [21, 23], highlighting the spread of practices through citizen participation. The acceleration of carbon neutrality practices through the full mandatory use of eco-friendly banners [16, 20, 22] and discussions on the International Court of Justice's advisory opinion on climate change [52, 55] underscore the importance of strengthening the policy foundation.

sections.title: 🌱 Expanding Renewable Energy and Accelerating Energy Innovation
sections.content: The renewable energy sector, a key driver in overcoming the climate crisis, is growing even faster through the spread of investment and technological innovation. Renewable energy investments are expanding, with renewable energy ETFs seeing a 65% surge in one-year returns [29] and H-Energy's 'Hetsal Green' surpassing 10,000 members [30]. This increase in renewable energy is shaping a 'low-day, high-night' electricity market, acting as a signal for electricity tariff reforms [32], and discussions on improving the compensation system for thermal power generation due to increased renewable energy [33, 37] are actively underway. Domestically, Gyeongsangbuk-do is pursuing the acquisition of carbon credits using renewable energy in Mongolia [35], and Jeonbuk Self-Governing Province is expanding energy innovation infrastructure linkages and renewable energy cooperation, centered around Saemangeum [36, 40], strengthening regional and international cooperation. As Australia innovates AI data centers with renewable energy [38] and nuclear fusion energy gains attention as a clean energy solution for climate change [58], energy innovation is accelerating in various fields, with K-nuclear power plants attempting to transform to adjust output like solar power [41].

sections.title: 🌍 Climate Change Adaptation and Ecosystem Response
sections.content: As the impacts of climate change intensify, multifaceted efforts are also underway to adapt and respond. Research has been published indicating that U.S. Everglades restoration efforts help save the planet from climate change [46], and in the North Kivu region of the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), agricultural practices are changing due to climate change [57]. New forms of adaptation strategies are being explored, such as discussions in El Salvador on wave protection measures through climate crisis insurance [66, 78]. News that King Penguins are successfully altering their breeding habits in the face of climate change [76] demonstrates the remarkable adaptability of natural ecosystems. Furthermore, scientific efforts continue, including research on hyperthermophilic microorganisms for climate change response and new antibiotic development [74], and research into the mechanisms of neural adaptation to climate change [54]. These diverse adaptation efforts and research will be crucial steps in finding solutions to the various challenges posed by climate change.

briefing.closing: Today's climate-related news has highlighted both a global crisis and the active, multifaceted efforts underway to overcome it. It is crucial to maintain vigilance regarding climate change and to continue taking sustained action. This concludes the daily climate briefing for March 17, 2026.

articles.title: Surfing’s big break: how climate crisis insurance may save El Salvador’s waves
articles.description: Fearing that extreme weather threatened its epic breaks, Oriente Salvaje is piloting the first surf insurance policy to protect livelihoods and ecosystemsIn the late 1990s in El Salvador, Rodrigo Barraza went in search of every surfer’s dream: a pristine wave, far from the crowds. articles.title: Saving the pint: behind the race to climate-proof beer in the US
articles.description: Water shortages and rising heat is putting pressure on beer ingredients, but US brewers and farmers are adaptingWith St Patrick’s Day this week, millions of Americans are raising a glass. Beer remains the country’s most popular alcoholic drink with more than 6bn gallons consumed each year. But from water shortages to rising temperatures, the climate crisis is putting pressure on beer’s most essential ingredients.At Deschutes Brewery in Bend, Oregon, beer is either stacked high in warehouse rows
